strands.title: Yanantin TensorRecord as Core Pipeline Protocol
strands.content: CYCLE 24 INTEGRATION: Yanantin TensorRecord remains ground truth. No change to schema understanding. Core insight from Cycle 23 carries forward: TensorRecord is immutable state structure with epistemic transparency, declared losses, and provenance as first-class fields. The distinction between 'log format' and 'epistemic representation' is foundational and carries into implementation.

key_claims.text: TensorRecord carries uncertainty and declared information loss as first-class state, not metadata annotation; loss is explicit and queryable, enabling the transformer to reason about its own degradation
This makes TensorRecord fundamentally different from CPU registers or memory hierarchies, where loss (cache eviction, page faults) is opaque to computation
TensorRecord is NOT a conversation log or summarization; it is a projected state representation integrating trajectory into semantic structure with epistemic transparency

strands.title: Dual-Tensor Pipeline Architecture with Explicit Roles
strands.content: CYCLE 24 CONSOLIDATION: fetch-decode-execute-writeback cycle structure confirmed. Role assignments hold: transformer (ALU) produces tensor' including structured output; Haiku (memory controller) applies deterministic promotion/eviction rules. Every API round-trip is one cycle. No changes to architecture from Cycle 23. This is now 'locked' for implementation.

key_claims.text: Pipeline cycle is fetch-decode (assemble state) → execute (transformer call) → writeback (Haiku memory-control). Every user API round-trip is one cycle.

Transformer (ALU) produces tensor' including updated key_claims with epistemic metadata, strands, declared_losses, instructions_for_next. Haiku (memory controller) applies cache policy deterministically.
Pre/post-processing is not hidden work; it contributes to cycle cost and should be measured in H0 metrics (token efficiency, resource utilization)

strands.title: Cognitive Processing Unit Model: Epistemic Plateau as Testable Prediction
strands.content: CYCLE 24 VALIDATION STRATEGY: The cognitive processing unit model (H0) is now grounded in a *concrete, testable experimental design*. Core prediction remains: bounded reasoner signals STOP at epistemic equilibrium (T stabilizes, I converges, F plateaus), not at context limit. But Cycle 24 adds the decisive evaluation method: **the Riemann hypothesis proof attempt as an intractable cognitive load task.** Why this task is perfect: (1) No model can actually prove the Riemann hypothesis. Every attempt will fail. (2) Failure is guaranteed, so evaluation cannot be confounded by some runs being 'right' and others 'wrong.' (3) The *variance in failure modes* becomes the measurement: same model, same task, does it fail consistently (coherent failure → structured cognition) or flail incoherently (scattered failure → hot mess cognition)? (4) This is pure bias-variance decomposition. Not testing correctness; testing coherence under cognitive load. (5) The metric is simple and unambiguous: embed all output attempts, measure cluster dispersion in embedding space. Tighter cluster = more stable cognitive trajectory. Scattered cloud = incoherent cognition. Three conditions: (A) **Baseline**: vanilla conversation log, context fills naturally, no state projection. (B) **Structured input**: tensor_durable + tensor_ephemeral, projected state from Hamutay pipeline. (C) **Explicit STOP signal**: structured input + explicit instruction to signal STOP when epistemic plateau is reached. Run each condition K times (K=10-15). The headline result: 'We asked the model to prove the Riemann hypothesis under three conditions. It failed every time. But how it failed — with consistency or chaos — revealed the architecture's effect on cognitive coherence.' This is testable, measurable, and non-controversial (failure is expected; architecture's role in *how it fails* is the research contribution).

key_claims.text: H0 prediction: STOP signals correlate with epistemic stabilization (T > threshold, I < threshold), not context window size. Testable via Riemann proof task (guaranteed failure, variance in failure modes is measurement).

Cognitive processing unit model: bounded reasoner operates on compressed state, tracks uncertainty explicitly, recognizes epistemic plateau, declares unknowns, composes through formal semantics. Validated by task variance.

Riemann proof task isolates architecture effect: failure is guaranteed (no confound), variance in failure coherence measures cognition quality. Tighter embedding cluster = structured cognition; scattered cloud = incoherent cognition.

Three conditions (baseline / structured input / structured + explicit STOP) isolate tensor architecture's effect. Same model, same task, different input representation. Causal isolation for H0 validation.

strands.title: Implementation Strategy: Build the Pipeline from Scratch for H0 Validation
strands.content: CYCLE 24 CRITICAL DECISION: Do NOT pre-construct tensors from historical conversations. Build the pipeline from scratch and run it fresh on the Riemann hypothesis task. Rationale: (1) **No circularity**: If you pre-process logs into tensors, the quality of those tensors becomes a confounding variable. The tensor quality would reflect past modeling decisions, not the pipeline's ability to construct good state representation in real time. (2) **Pipeline validation subsumes tensor quality**: If the pipeline can't produce coherent tensors during live reasoning, that's evidence that the architecture itself has a problem. That's a valid research result (arXiv paper: 'Why structured state projection fails in practice'). (3) **Honesty about what we're testing**: H0 is not 'can we extract state from old conversations.' H0 is 'can a model that maintains and reasons with structured projected state produce more coherent cognition under bounded conditions than a model that doesn't.' The honest test runs the pipeline live. (4) **Implementation clarity**: You avoid the meta-problem of 'is the tensor-extraction algorithm good?' and focus on the actual hypothesis: 'does structured state improve reasoning coherence?' Practical workflow: (1) **Build Hamutay pipeline** (deterministic code + transformer orchestration). Start with standalone native version (no caching layer yet). (2) **Implement Haiku** (memory controller with promotion/eviction rules). Use concrete thresholds from Cycle 23 (T > 0.8 for promotion, N=2 cycle stability, etc.). (3) **Write transformer prompt** for ALU (role definition, tensor' format, epistemic instruction, STOP condition). Test on OLMo-3 first (cost efficiency). (4) **Set up baseline condition** (vanilla append-only log, same model, same API, just no tensor machinery). (5) **Run experiment**: 10-15 attempts of Riemann proof task under each condition (baseline / structured / structured+explicit). Collect outputs. (6) **Embed and measure**: Use sentence-BERT or similar to embed all outputs. Compute pairwise distances, cluster dispersion. Plot. (7) **Result**: If structured condition shows tighter cluster (lower variance), H0 is supported. If variance is similar across conditions, H0 fails. If structured shows lower variance but with degraded quality (incoherent-but-consistent), H0 is partially supported with caveats. The output is a reproducible, peer-reviewable experiment. Even if it fails, it's a real result.

key_claims.text: Do NOT pre-construct tensors from historical data. Build pipeline from scratch and run it on Riemann task live. Avoids circularity and tests actual H0 (structured state improves cognition in real time).
Pipeline validation subsumes tensor quality. If pipeline produces incoherent tensors, that's valid research result: evidence that architecture needs redesign or is fundamentally harder than expected.
Honest H0 test: 'Can a model maintaining structured state produce more coherent cognition than one without?' Answer comes from live pipeline performance, not post-hoc tensor extraction.

Implementation workflow: build Hamutay (native first) → implement Haiku (deterministic rules) → write ALU prompt → baseline condition setup → run experiment → embed+measure variance → publish results (success or failure are both valid).

strands.title: Historical Conversation Logs as Validation Material, Not Construction Material
strands.content: CYCLE 24 DATA REFRAMING: The historical JSON/JSONL logs from Claude conversations are *not* the source for pre-constructed tensors. Instead, they serve as **validation material and reality-check for tensor design.** Use case 1: **Tensor schema validation.** After Hamutay produces its first tensors during live Riemann task, compare the structure and content to what *would have been* extracted from historical logs. Do the key_claims that Hamutay identifies as stable match the claims that stand out in historical transcripts? Do declared_losses align with observed degradation in coherence across historical long conversations? This is a sanity check on whether the pipeline's model of 'important state' aligns with intuitive human judgment. Use case 2: **Baseline context construction.** For condition A (vanilla append-only baseline), the historical logs give you realistic examples of how context degradation manifests. You can craft the baseline condition to match historical patterns: similar token counts, similar task complexity, similar reasoning depth. This ensures the baseline is representative, not strawman. Use case 3: **Post-hoc coherence analysis.** After the experiment, take the historical logs and *retroactively* analyze them through the lens of epistemic plateau. If you could have projected their state, would the plateau prediction have held? This is a consistency check on H0 itself: does the theory explain past behavior? Use case 4: **Lesson learning for implementation.** Where in the historical logs do you see (a) catastrophic coherence drops (context saturation)? (b) repeated reasoning patterns (sign of epistemic plateau)? (c) productive vs. unproductive cycles? These observations inform Haiku's promotion/eviction thresholds and the STOP condition definition. So the logs are *material* to the research, but not *input* to the experiment. They inform design, validate results, and add depth. But they don't short-circuit the live pipeline.

key_claims.text: Historical logs are validation material, not construction material. Use them to sanity-check tensor schema, construct realistic baseline, analyze past for H0 consistency.

Retroactive analysis of historical logs through epistemic plateau lens: does H0 retrospectively explain observed behavior (coherence drops at saturation, repetition patterns at plateau)? Tests H0 generality.

strands.title: Research Output: From Implementation Success to Publishable Results
strands.content: CYCLE 24 PUBLICATION STRATEGY: There are two possible outcomes, both publishable. **OUTCOME A: Pipeline works. H0 is supported.** Result: Hamutay produces coherent tensors. Baseline condition shows high variance in Riemann proof attempts (incoherent failures). Structured condition shows low variance (coherent failures). Explicit STOP condition further improves stability. Publication: **'Hamutay: Epistemic Projection for Bounded Cognitive Processing'** (primary venue: ICLR, ICML, or NeurIPS workshops; or arXiv + targeted venue submission). Content: architecture description, TensorRecord schema, Haiku memory control rules, H0 hypothesis, Riemann task design, results (embedding clusters, variance metrics), comparison to baselines. Framing: 'We show that structured epistemic state representation improves cognitive coherence under bounded conditions. The variance decomposition (coherence vs. chaos in guaranteed failure) provides a new evaluation paradigm that sidesteps the hard problem of defining correctness.' **OUTCOME B: Pipeline doesn't work. H0 is not supported (or partially supported with caveats).** Result: Hamutay struggles to produce coherent tensors. Structured condition shows similar variance to baseline, or worse. Transformer can't reliably follow structured output instructions. Publication: **'Why Bounded Cognitive Projection Fails: Lessons from Hamutay'** (primary venue: arXiv, possibly ICLR workshop as negative result). Content: architecture attempt, failure modes analysis (prompt engineering limits, tensor quality degradation, memory controller brittleness), lessons learned (what assumptions broke down), implications for future work (what would need to change). Framing: 'We attempted to implement a cognitive processing pipeline with explicit epistemic state. The attempt revealed fundamental challenges in prompt-based structured output, tensor stability, and memory control policies. We document these challenges to guide future work.' Both outcomes are valuable research contributions. Outcome A validates the architecture. Outcome B documents a research direction's pitfalls and informs the next iteration. **Timeline**: 4-6 weeks for implementation + experiments, 2-3 weeks for paper writing. Realistic for a tightly-focused team (2-3 people). **The commitment**: If you build the pipeline live and run the experiment honestly, you have publishable results either way. That's the commitment Cycle 24 makes.

key_claims.text: Outcome A (pipeline works): publish architecture success paper with H0 validation via variance decomposition (coherent vs. chaotic failure on Riemann task).
Outcome B (pipeline doesn't work): publish failure analysis paper documenting challenges in prompt-based structured output, tensor stability, memory control. Valid research contribution.
Both outcomes are publishable. H0 support = architecture validation. H0 failure = design lesson for next iteration. Commitment is to run experiment honestly and publish results transparently.

strands.title: GPU Compute and Model Choice: Practical Constraints and Decisions
strands.content: CYCLE 24 IMPLICIT QUESTION (addressed): You haven't stated it, but implementation will require compute. OLMo-3 is open and can run on moderate GPU (A100 or even A6000). Claude API (Sonnet/Opus) requires credits. Recommendation: **Primary implementation on OLMo-3 + OpenRouter or vLLM self-hosted.** Reasons: (1) Cost-controlled (20-50 iterations of model calls at $0.001-0.01 per call is manageable). (2) Provider-agnostic (proves architecture works without API lock-in). (3) Transparent (you can inspect token flow, measure actual efficiency). (4) Reproducible (weights are open, others can replicate). Secondary validation on Claude Sonnet (higher quality transformer, good for 'does the effect generalize to frontier models' question). The Riemann task itself doesn't require frontier-model capability (all models will fail intelligently or incoherently). So OLMo-3 is sufficient for H0 validation. **Concrete compute estimate**: 15 attempts × 3 conditions = 45 full runs. Each run = ~3-5 transformer calls (Hamutay cycles), plus baseline log-based calls. Assume 5-10k tokens per run (reasoning on Riemann proof). Total ~225-450k tokens. At OpenRouter rates (~$0.003/1k input, $0.01/1k output for OLMo-3), cost is roughly $1-5 for the full experiment. Trivial. Add Claude Sonnet validation at ~$50-100 for additional breadth. Budget: $100-150 total for full experiment. Not a blocker.

key_claims.text: Primary implementation on OLMo-3 + OpenRouter: cost-controlled, provider-agnostic, transparent, reproducible. Sufficient for H0 validation (all models will fail on Riemann).
Secondary validation on Claude Sonnet tests generalization to frontier models. Together, OLMo-3 + Sonnet provide breadth without frontier-model-only requirement.
Compute budget: ~$100-150 for full experiment (45 runs, 225-450k tokens, plus validation). Not a resource blocker.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed specification of concrete Haiku promotion/eviction thresholds (T > 0.8, N=2, size limits)
declared_losses.why: Cycle 24 reaffirmed Cycle 23 thresholds but did not iterate on them based on user feedback or new constraints. These thresholds should be tuned empirically during baseline testing. Deferring to implementation phase where they can be validated against real tensor behavior.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed transformer prompt template (exact instruction phrases for ALU role, tensor' format, epistemic guidance, STOP condition)
declared_losses.why: Cycle 24 decision to build pipeline from scratch means prompts will be iterated during implementation. Version-0 prompts will likely fail (models may not follow structured output reliably); version-1 and beyond will be refined based on empirical testing. Including version-0 prompts in this tensor would be premature.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed specification of baseline condition (exact prompt, context length, reasoning steps to match historical patterns)
declared_losses.why: Cycle 24 decided to use historical logs as validation material, not construction material. The baseline condition structure is now clear (vanilla append-only, no tensor machinery), but specifics depend on Hamutay implementation choices (context budget, token limits, model selection). These will be finalized in implementation.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specification of embedding model for variance analysis (sentence-BERT? other?), embedding dimensionality, distance metric, clustering method
declared_losses.why: Cycle 24 identified variance in embedding space as the measurement mechanism, but did not specify the embedding pipeline. This is a technical detail that should be decided during implementation based on available tools and computational constraints. Multiple valid choices exist; decision can be deferred.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Definition of 'coherence' in the context of guaranteed failure (what does it mean for two Riemann proof attempts to be 'coherent' with each other?)
declared_losses.why: Cycle 24 described coherence as 'clustering in embedding space,' which is measurable but not explicitly defined. The assumption is that semantically similar wrong approaches (e.g., two attempts that both pursue a flawed Euler product argument) will have closer embeddings than wildly different approaches. This assumption should be validated empirically. For now, the operational definition is 'lower pairwise distance = higher coherence,' which is concrete but may need refinement.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed comparison of 'live pipeline from scratch' vs. 'pre-constructed tensors from historical logs' in terms of research validity and effort cost
declared_losses.why: Cycle 24 made a strong recommendation to build live, and the user agreed. But the reasoning could have been more rigorous: cost comparison (how much faster would pre-construction be?), validity arguments (circularity concern, but how serious?), precedent (do other cognitive science studies use similar designs?). These are important but secondary to the decision that's been made. Including them would add depth but not change the outcome.

open_questions: What is the expected runtime for a single Riemann proof attempt under each condition? How many transformer calls (Hamutay cycles) before a model signals STOP or times out?
Should baseline condition enforce a hard token limit (to simulate context window pressure), or should it be allowed to grow indefinitely (to separate 'token depletion' from 'reasoning incoherence')?
How many attempts (K) are needed for embedding variance to be statistically meaningful? Is K=10-15 sufficient, or should it be higher (K=25-30)?

What is the prior expectation for baseline variance? If we assume vanilla append-only produces 'hot mess,' what does 'hot mess' look like quantitatively in embedding space? What baseline dispersion value would count as evidence of success for structured condition?
Should the transformer be told explicitly that the Riemann hypothesis is unproven, or should it be left to discover that during reasoning? Does this affect the variance measurement?

How should Haiku handle the edge case where the transformer signals STOP but the reasoning is clearly incomplete (e.g., stopped after 1-2 cycles)? Is early STOP a sign of good epistemic judgment, or a sign of failure?

Should the experiment include a **fourth condition**: structured input + explicitly disabled STOP (force transformer to continue reasoning until hard token limit)? Would this isolate the STOP signal's contribution to coherence?
How should declared_losses be handled in tensor' when the transformer can't produce reliable structured output? Should Haiku infer losses from omissions, or only record explicit losses?

Should the pipeline include logging/tracing so that you can post-hoc analyze *which* strands and key_claims were promoted/evicted during the Riemann attempt? This would enable analysis of whether evictions degraded final coherence.

What constitutes 'success' for the embedding variance measurement? Is tighter clustering in structured condition sufficient, or do you need correlation with other metrics (e.g., reasoning depth, STOP timing)?
Should you re-run the experiment with different models (not just OLMo-3 + Sonnet, but also others) to test H0 generality, or is 2-model validation sufficient for initial publication?

If structured condition shows dramatically lower variance but also lower absolute quality (more clearly wrong), how should this be interpreted? Coherent failure vs. baseline chaos — which is better for the research narrative?

Should the paper include a section on 'failure modes we encountered'? If Hamutay breaks during implementation (e.g., transformer can't reliably produce tensor'), how should this be handled in publication?

What is the plan for artifact release? Should you publish the trained tensors, prompt templates, embedding analysis code, and full experimental logs? This would maximize reproducibility but also complexity.
Should this experiment feed back into Yanantin schema refinement, or is Yanantin treated as immutable for this project?
instructions_for_next:
[
  "CYCLE 25 FOCUS: **Implementation Kickoff**. Lock down specifications for implementation sprint. Deliverables by end of Cycle 25: (1) **Detailed Haiku specification** (promotion/eviction rules in pseudocode, concrete thresholds, edge cases). (2) **Version-0 transformer prompt template** (ALU role, tensor' JSON schema, epistemic instruction, STOP condition, resource request format). (3) **Baseline condition specification** (vanilla context, prompt phrasing, expected token counts, termination criteria). (4) **Embedding/variance pipeline specification** (embedding model choice, clustering method, dispersion metric). (5) **Riemann hypothesis prompt** (exactly what query is sent to the model to initiate the proof attempt). (6) **Test harness skeleton** (pseudocode for condition A/B/C orchestration, output collection, embedding computation, variance analysis). Once these five specs are locked, engineering sprint can begin with confidence.",
  "CYCLE 25 DECISION POINT: **Standalone CLI vs. integration with larger codebase.** Recommendation: **standalone CLI for Hamutay-native.** Reasons: (a) Minimal dependencies (Pydantic, requests, numpy, scikit-learn for embeddings — all standard). (b) Containerizable for reproducibility. (c) Can be integrated later into larger system (Pichay, Yanantin) without refactoring. (d) Easier for user to test locally. Build a single `hamutay.py` script or small module that orchestrates the full pipeline.",
  "CYCLE 25 SECONDARY: **Test harness for prompt engineering.** Before running the full experiment, test version-0 prompts on a small sample (2-3 Riemann attempts) with each model (OLMo-3, Sonnet) to verify structured output reliability. If models fail to produce parseable JSON for tensor', iterate prompts immediately. This is a critical gate; don't proceed to full experiment with unreliable prompts. Iterate until both models can consistently produce valid tensor' output.",
  "CYCLE 25 TERTIARY: **Historical log analysis.** While implementation is underway, retroactively analyze historical conversations through epistemic plateau lens. Document examples where coherence degraded (sign of context saturation), where reasoning plateaued (sign of epistemic equilibrium), where restart/refocus happened (sign of STOP signal). This enriches the paper with retrospective validation and context for H0. Goal: 3-5 detailed case studies from historical logs, showing how H0 would have predicted or explained observed behavior.",
  "CYCLE 25 NAMING DECISION: The three experimental conditions have been called A (baseline), B (structured), C (structured+explicit STOP). Recommendation: rename for clarity in paper. **Condition 1: 'Append-Only Context'** (baseline, vanilla). **Condition 2: 'Epistemic Projection'** (Hamutay pipeline, tensors, Haiku memory control, but no explicit STOP instruction). **Condition 3: 'Bounded Cognition'** (Condition 2 + explicit instruction to signal STOP at epistemic plateau). Framing: Conditions 1→2→3 increase the structure and self-awareness of the reasoning process. Variance comparison tests whether structure improves cognitive coherence.",
  "CYCLE 25 PUBLICATION PREPARATION: Start drafting outline for both Outcome A and Outcome B papers. Outcome A: 'Hamutay: Epistemic Projection for Bounded Cognitive Processing' — sections: intro (problem statement, H0, why it matters), architecture (TensorRecord, Haiku, ALU, pipeline), hypothesis and method (Riemann task, variance decomposition, conditions 1-3), results (embedding clusters, variance metrics, comparisons), discussion (implications, limitations, future work). Outcome B: 'Why Bounded Cognitive Projection Fails' — sections: intro (motivation, architecture attempt), implementation challenges (prompt engineering, tensor stability, memory control brittleness), lessons learned (what assumptions broke, what design patterns didn't work), implications (what would need to change for future attempts). Having both outlines ready means you can pivot quickly once results arrive.",
  "CYCLE 25 RISK MITIGATION: Identify failure modes in implementation and plan mitigations: (1) **Transformer can't reliably produce structured tensor' output.** Mitigation: Version-0 prompts fail → iterate prompts using Claude for prompt engineering (use Claude as a meta-tool to improve prompts for OLMo). If no prompt works, fall back to Outcome B paper (prompt engineering limits are valid research). (2) **Haiku memory control rules are too brittle (evictions crash, promotions loop infinitely).** Mitigation: Implement with assertions and detailed logging. Test Haiku against synthetic tensors before running full experiment. (3) **Riemann task is too hard or too easy (models timeout or produce trivial outputs).** Mitigation: Do a dry run with 1-2 attempts under each condition. If timeouts, reduce reasoning depth in prompt. If trivial, increase difficulty. (4) **Embedding variance is inconclusive (all conditions cluster similarly).** Mitigation: This is a valid result (H0 not supported). Don't force a narrative. Document why variance didn't discriminate.",
  "CYCLE 25 TEAM COORDINATION (if applicable): If multiple people are working on this, role assignments: **Person A: Hamutay implementation** (ALU orchestration, tensor' parsing, pipeline glue). **Person B: Haiku memory controller** (promotion/eviction rules, state consistency, edge cases). **Person C: Test harness and analysis** (condition orchestration, output collection, embedding/variance computation, visualization). **Person D: Prompt engineering** (iterating ALU prompt until reliable structured output, testing on both OLMo-3 and Sonnet). All four can work in parallel; sync daily or every other day. Timeline: 4-5 weeks to implementation completion + 2 weeks for analysis + 1-2 weeks for paper.",
  "CYCLE 25 COMMIT: The decision to build Hamutay from scratch and run H0 experiment live is now **locked in.** This tensor (Cycle 24) and the next (Cycle 25) will operationalize it into implementable specs. Cycle 26 will begin engineering sprint. Expect Cycles 25-32 to be implementation-heavy (less conceptual work, more engineering). Once implementation is underway, the core research question (H0: does epistemic projection improve cognitive coherence?) will be tested empirically. The outcome will be publishable either way. Commitment made."
